statusLine: European fuel security is under strain with 17 of 27 EU countries critically short on at least one fuel type, despite adequate overall reserves.
fullAnalysis:
While EU-wide averages remain above the mandatory 90-day minimum for petrol (94.5 days) and diesel (95.8 days), the distribution reveals significant vulnerabilities. Seventeen countries face critical shortages in at least one fuel category, with jet fuel showing particular weakness at an EU average of just 89.9 days. Romania presents the most severe case, with all three fuel types critically low, including diesel stocks of only 20.2 days.

Regional patterns emerge from the data, with Eastern European nations like Poland, Romania, and Lithuania among the most vulnerable, while Nordic countries generally maintain stronger reserves. The jet fuel situation is especially concerning, with 19 of 27 countries below safe levels, reflecting both higher consumption from aviation recovery and supply chain pressures from the ongoing Middle East disruptions.

Fuel price data shows significant variation across the bloc, with Dutch consumers paying €2.334 per liter for petrol compared to Malta's €1.34, indicating different tax structures and supply dynamics. The recent 0.75% decline in Brent crude to $95.2 per barrel provides some relief, though prices remain elevated at 27% above pre-conflict levels, maintaining pressure on both reserves and consumer costs.
keyPoints: 17 of 27 EU countries have critical shortages in at least one fuel type, despite meeting bloc-wide minimums
Jet fuel shows the greatest vulnerability with 19 countries below safe levels and EU average at 89.9 days
Romania faces the most severe situation with all fuels critical, including diesel at only 20.2 days
Eastern European countries show higher vulnerability than Nordic nations, with Poland and Lithuania also facing multiple critical shortages
Fuel price disparities remain significant, ranging from €1.34 to €2.334 per liter for petrol across member states
